Fern Polrrrcar PRACrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0556
September 4,2007
Naaz Alikhan
1512 Sheridan Avenue
Roseville, CA9566l
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-07-146
Dear Ms. Alikhan:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a member of
the Roseville Design Re-vrew Committee under the conflict-of-interisi provisions of the political
Reform Act (the "Ac]L').l Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with
the immunityprovided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Seciion g3ll4;Regulation
I 8329 (c)(3), copy enclosed.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, oiother conflict-of-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. For example, governmental
agencies may maintain lists of "incompatible activities," which are not part of the Act. you
should consult your City Attorney to ensure that you do not engage in outside employment that
Roseville has determined to be incompatible with your responsibilities to the city.
QUESTTON
Does the Act restrict you from accepting private employment while serving as a public
official?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government code Se_ctions g1000 through glol4. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the i'ai. political practices
CommissionarecontainedinSections 18ll0throughl8gg7 ofTitlezofttrJCaurorniaCodeolRegulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless ottrerwise indicated.
g


File No. I-07-146
Page No. 2
You are a community member serving on the Design Review Committee in Roseville. In
your private capacity, you are an interior designer working for a local architectural firm. Last
year, an applicant came before the committee regarding a building design. you had previously
(approximately 6 years ago) performed work for the client but have noiperformed any work for
him since that time. The committee, with your affirmative vote, upprorrid the project. 1.n.
applicant has now come to you in your private capacity requesting your interior design .ori""r.,
You have asked whether you may accept this private employment
ANALYSIS
There is nothing in the Act that prohibits a public official from accepting outside
employment. Thus you acceptance^of the offer to perform design services, in itself does not
implicate the provisions of the Act.3 Under the Ac1, however, outside employment may
disqualiff yotftomfuture governmental decisions that affect a client. The Commission has
developed an eight-step process for deciding whether an official has a disquali$ring conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision; we generally describe this process Ueto,i. (Regulation
18700(bx1)-(8).)
Steps 1. and 2- Are you a public official who may make, participate in making, or use your
official position to influence a governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials.,, (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation- 18700(b)(1).) '?ublic official" is defined as "everymernber, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agency...." (Section tiO+A.1 A ,llocal
government agenct''means a county, city or district of any kind, including any county board or
commission. (Section 82041.) As a member of the city's design review "J--itt.", you are a
"public official" for purposes of the Act (see Sections 82041 *rA SZO+11, and the conflict of
interest rules apply to you.
CONCLUSION
The Act does not bar you from acceptingprivate employment; however, outside
employrnentmay disqualifu you from participating in future Design Review Committee
decisions that affect your client.
FACTS
2 we assume from the facts you presentthat, at the time you voted on the project affecting the applicant,
you had not received a promise of future income from, were not nigotiating future "."pfoy-""t with, and did not
have any arrangement for future employnent with, the applicant. (See, SeJtions g7103(ci u"aAZ+01.1
3 Please be aware that other laws, outside the jurisdiction of the Fair Political practices Commission
("Commission') may restrict the ability of a public omtiat to accept this employment, if it is determined that the
actwity is "incompatible"' The Commission's advice is limited to matters arising under the Act. (Regulation
18329(bX8Xd)') we urge you to check with your city attorney to determine if there are other taws ttra't may be
applicable in light ofthe facts you present.


File No. I-07-146
Page No. 3
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only where a public official ,,make[s],
partrcipate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his official position to influence a
govefirmental decision in which he knows orhas reason to know he has afrnancialinterest.,,
(Section 87100; Reeulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series of Regulations
which define "making,"."participating in makiug,,, and.,influencing; u gorr"*-ental dicision,
and which provide certain exceptions to these actions. (Regulationi rclOz-tg702.4;
Making or Participattng in Making a Governmental Decision: Apublic official..makes a
govemmental decision" when the official, actingwithin the authority of his or her office or
position, votes on amatter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 1g702.i.) A
public official "participates in making a govemmental decisioi" *h".r, uriirg within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or interveiing review, the
official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding ihe
governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) When, as a member of the design reviJw board,
you review plans for possible approval, you will be making a govemmental decision. you are
also "participating" in a government decision when yo, ur" iniolved in making
recommendations to the other city bodies.
Using Your Official Positton to Influence a Governmental Decision: public officials are
also prohibited from "influencing" a governmental decision. There are two rules as to whether a
public official uses or attempts to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or bettore the public
official's own agency or an ager..cy appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public
official's agency, but the public official is not a decision-makerper ."I 1n"g.rt ation 1g7023(a).)
In that case, if "the official contacts, or appezlrs before, or otheriise attempi's to influenc ",;; 
'
member, officer, employee or consultantof the agenct'' then he or she is attempting to iniluence
a governmental decision. This includes, but is not limited to,'oappeararrces or contacts by the
official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.,, (Ibid.i
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency
other than the public official's own agency, or an agerrcy appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the public official's agency. (R.egulation 1g702.3(b).) under this rule, the
official cannot act or purport "to act on behalf oq or as the representative oq his or her agencv to
any member, offi.cer, employee or consultant of an agency''to influence a decision.t (Ibid)-' *
a Regulation l87o2.4provides several exceptions to the definition discussed above. For example, there is
an exception for the presentation of architectural, engineering, or similar drawings by the official to his or her own
agency so long as the official has no other direct oral or writtin contact with the-agency, except for necessary
contact with agency staff conceming the processing or evaluation of the drawings-or .rrb*irrioo, prepared by the
official' In addition, there isan exception for appearances before a desip or architectural review committee or
similar body of which the official is a member to present drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or
similar nature which the official has prepared for a client if the fo-ltowing tlree criteria are met: (A) The review
committee's sole function is to review architectural or engineering plans or desigrs and to make recommendations
in that instance concerning those plans or desigrs to a planning co-mmission o, o-th", agency; 1n; rne ordinance or


File No. I-07-146
Page No. 4
Based on your facts, this factor could be met with respect to future applications before
your agency of the client.
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
economic interests. A public official has an economic interest in:
o A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirects investment of $2,000 or more
(Section 87103(a); Regulation I 8703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer,
patlner, trustee, employee or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. I (b));
o Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section
87 1 03(b); Regulation lS7 03.2);
o Any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision (Section s7103(c); Regulation tizo:.:);
' 
Any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation 1g703.4);
o His or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the
"personal financial effects" rule. (section g7103; Regulation 1s703.5.)
We assume your prospective client will be a source of income to you of $500 or more. In
addition, you also have an interest in your personal finances and tlose of your immediate family.
(Regulation 18703.5.) A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if
the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his
or her immediate family increasing or decreasing. You have not specifically referenced any
other economic interests; therefore, we limit our discussion to yogr economic interest in your
client as a source of income.
other provision of law requires that the review committee
professions, and the official was appointed to the body to
practitioner.
include architects, engineers orpersons in related
fulfill this requirement; and (C) The official is a sole
5 For purposes of Section 87lO3,"indirect investnent or interest means any inveshnent or interest owned
by the spouse or dependent child ofa public official, by an agent on behalfofa puuii" ornciui, o, uy a business
entity or trust in which the official, the official's ag,e1ts, spo,rr", and dependent 
"t itar"., own jirectiy, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." A public official's "immediate family', includes only the official,s
spouse and dependent children. (Section B2O2g.)


File No. I-07-146
Page No. 5
Step 4. Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be
determined if the official's economic interest is directlyinvolved or indirectly
involved in the goveflrmental decision." (Regulation tbzo+1a;.;
The answer to this question determines the level at which an effect of a given decision on
the economic interest will be deemed "material." Because we do not have full information on
the potential governmental decisions in which you may be confronted with, we provide you with
the language of Regulation18704.1, which wili assist you in making this dltermination in the
future as the details ofparticular decisions become known:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agencywhen that
person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an applicatron, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2)Js a named parry in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is th-e subject of-a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewaf approval, denial, or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
With respect to personal financial effects, Regulation 18704.5 provides that apublic
official or his or her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a governmental
decision that has any financial effect on the personal finances of the public oflcial or his or her
immediate family. This might be the case should the governmental decision affect your future
contracts with the client.
Steps 5' and 6. will there be a material and foreseeable financial effect on your economic
interests?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether the decision will have a material financial effect on any of tirose economic
interests. The official must find the applicable materiality standard in Ctmmission Regulations.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(bX5), Regulation 18765, er seq.) In addition, the official
must determine if a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. An effect upon economic
interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it wifl
occur' (Regulation 18706(a)') Whether the financial consequences of a govemmental decision
are substantially likely at the time the decision is made depends on the faits surrounding the
decision. A financial effect need not be certain to be cons-idered reasonably foreseeable, but it


File No. I-07-146
Page No. 6
must be more than a mere possibility. (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC ops.
le8.)
Generally, where a source of income is directly involved in a decision as an applicant or
named patty, materiality is presumed and disqualificaiion is required. With respect to the
indirect standard, the applicable materiality standard varies depending on the nature of the
source.
Indirectly Involved Business Entities: For business entities that are indirectly involved in
a govemmental decision, Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards. Subdivision
(cX4) of this regulation applies to small business-entities, and if any of ine business entity
economic interests identified above meet the following criteria in this subdivision, the financial
effects of the decisions will be ,,mateljal,,:
"(A) The goveflrmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $ 20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $ 5,000 o, -ori; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of th: business entity's assets or liabilities of $ 2O,O0O or more.,,
(Regulation 1 8705. 1 (c)(a).)
Sources of Income who are individuals; Under Regulation 18705.3, an effect of a
decision is material as to an individual who is a source of income to an official if any of the
following applies:
"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) ty $
1,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title 2, California iode of Regulations,
sections 18705.2(b)),
Personal Finances: An impact on an official's personal finances is considered ,.material,,
if it amounts to $ 250 or more in any twelve-month period. (Regulation 1s705.5.)


File No. I-07-146
Page No. 7
Steps 7. and,8. Exceptions
- 
Even if you determine that adecision would have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of your economic interests, you may.tilt purti"ipate in the
decision if the effect on your interests is not distinguishablofrom the effect on tle public
generally. Additionally, in certairlrare circumstances, you may be ,,legally required,, to
participate in a decision. Since we do not know what kinds of decisioris oithe committee might
arise with respect to your client, we can only provide this general discussion of the exceptionL
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
Jwwjl

Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0556 September 4,2007 Naaz Alikhan 1512 Sheridan Avenue Roseville, CA9566l Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our FiIe No. I-07-146 Dear Ms. Alikhan: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as a member of the Roseville Design Re-vrew Committee under the conflict-of-interisi provisions of the political Reform Act (the "Ac]L').l Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunityprovided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Seciion g3ll4;Regulation I 8329 (c)(3), copy enclosed.) Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, oiother conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. For example, governmental agencies may maintain lists of "incompatible activities," which are not part of the Act. you should consult your City Attorney to ensure that you do not engage in outside employment that Roseville has determined to be incompatible with your responsibilities to the city. QUESTTON Does the Act restrict you from accepting private employment while serving as a public official? ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government code Se_ctions g1000 through glol4. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the i'ai. political practices CommissionarecontainedinSections 18ll0throughl8gg7 ofTitlezofttrJCaurorniaCodeolRegulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless ottrerwise indicated. g File No. I-07-146 Page No. 2 You are a community member serving on the Design Review Committee in Roseville. In your private capacity, you are an interior designer working for a local architectural firm. Last year, an applicant came before the committee regarding a building design. you had previously (approximately 6 years ago) performed work for the client but have noiperformed any work for him since that time. The committee, with your affirmative vote, upprorrid the project. 1.n. applicant has now come to you in your private capacity requesting your interior design .ori""r., You have asked whether you may accept this private employment ANALYSIS There is nothing in the Act that prohibits a public official from accepting outside employment. Thus you acceptance^of the offer to perform design services, in itself does not implicate the provisions of the Act.3 Under the Ac1, however, outside employment may disqualiff yotftomfuture governmental decisions that affect a client. The Commission has developed an eight-step process for deciding whether an official has a disquali$ring conflict of interest in a given governmental decision; we generally describe this process Ueto,i. (Regulation 18700(bx1)-(8).) Steps 1. and 2- Are you a public official who may make, participate in making, or use your official position to influence a governmental decision?
